Problem is caused by the fact that the babel compiler is UTF8 javascript, so when dukpy tries to read the compiler to run it it will receive utf8 data, but Python3.X decodes data according to the system locale.

Your system locale is probably ascii ("C"?) so Python is trying to decode the babel source code as plain ascii and failing.

I hope to fix this for next release as it's also a requirement for windows systems

I'll let folks coming to look at this know how I finally installed the latest version of `pew`: I used `pipsi` (https://github.com/mitsuhiko/pipsi). It creates a separate virtualenv for each package that you install, and exposes the binaries. This is a great way to avoid system conflicts that can't be resolved by simply using `pip --user`.

Regarding

> The tests before looked like they were passing but they were not actually. Tox couldn't find the correct versions of python since all tests were set to use 3.4 versions of python. Tox would gracefully exit giving a message that it couldn't find the right version of python and then, boom, exit code 0. I've removed tox on travis, in favor of running pytest directly in parallel with all the other versions of python. This is faster and more accurate. The windows tests still use tox, as all versions of python are installed on the VM they spin up when you test so tox works well.

It's not quite true that

> tests were set to use 3.4 versions of python

That was just the version picked to install tox on top (which was the most recent version supported by Travis at the time), in fact you can see that in this same year Travis was running tests again 3.3:

https://travis-ci.org/berdario/pew/jobs/195415325

The exit code 0 was intentional, as you can see here:

https://github.com/berdario/pew/commit/d1660e5add95839fd89c33b61e75dbab71a9ce91

I originally tried to add Python3.5, but Travis CI was lagging behind, and I decided to keep it enabled, but ignore it.... My rationale was that as soon as Travis would've enabled Python3.5, tests would've started to run against it

But apparently Travis changed the way that they now provide Python3 versions (instead of installing all the available ones)

(which is actually cleaner and better, though it breaks the expectation that we had of Travis until now :/ )

Hey @DanMayhem, thanks for bringing my old PR up to speed.

I've been using a `.inve.ini` to inject variables into my pew environments, but the more I think about it, the cruftier it seems. The string interpolation is crufty: trying to interpolate a variable that does not exist simply passes it along instead of erroring out or being replaced by the empty string. Overall, the INI setup seems too focused on injecting a simple set of variables without any support for logic or other more complicated setups.

I think the approach in #3 is actually the way to move forward. Allow the user to define a python module somewhere under `$VIRTUAL_ENV` that pew can source. pew can just define a number of function names that it calls as hooks, and the `inve` hook can simply inject things into `os.environ` to get all the functionality in the INI approach.

@kungfu71186 

> `def ipv4_private(self, network=False, address_class=None, include_other=false):`

My issue with that is that right now `ipv4` can proxy _all_ kind of generated IPs. If we extend just `ipv4_private` with new method parameter, it will no longer be the case. If we extend `ipv4` method too - it will become bulky.

But that aside, I wonder if there is a valid use case for generating IPs from the other ranges you mention. The term _private IP_ is not really well defined. Some of them are for documentation purposes, some for operator grade NAT, some are restricted for other purposes, their purpose does not overlap. Most of those other ranges should not really be used and I'm not sure there is a valid use case for generating them randomly from one big bucket (`include_others`). You'd sometimes randomly get a documentation IP, sometimes link local, and sometimes... a truly reserved one.

```python

However still throws the exception. I thought that was a bit funny so tried making the request with curl:

```
curl -v -u 'email:password' https://domain.zendesk.com/api/v2/macros/360015276352.json   -H "Content-Type: application/json" -X PUT -d '{"macro": {"restrictions": {}}}'

It still throws a 400. Looking through the curl output I noticed this strange line:

```
< X-Zendesk-API-Warn: Removed restricted keys ["macro.restrictions"] from parameters according to whitelist

After a bit of digging I discovered that the correct attribute name is `restriction`, and that is why it is failing. Now I am able to set the restriction to null via curl, however if I try to set it to an empty dict I get a 500 internal server error.

dlib::fatal_error should only be thrown for when an unrecoverable error has occurred.  Have you seen it come out of dlib in a case that you think was recoverable?  Where?  

Here is another example:

```matrix<double> m(3,3);
m(6,7) = 9;
```
What should happen?  

The reason DLIB_ASSERT throws an exception rather than calling assert()/abort() the first time is because in many environments the diagnostic output of doing that is much less clear or more difficult to trace than an exception being thrown.  However, the second check is there to catch people who are trying to ignore non-recoverable errors like the m(6,7) = 9 above.

You are missing the point of the fatal_error.  It's not indicating that an error has been detected in dlib. It's telling you that dlib has detected an error in the calling application.  Just like when the operating system detects an error in your application when you go out of bounds of an array, it terminates that application with a message instructing you to fix your application.

For posterity, here's a bit more detail on how t-measures can be computed more efficiently.

After computing the least-common-ancestor matrices for `ref` and `est`, we iterate over query frames `q`.

For each `q`, we slice `ref_scores = lca_ref[q, window]` and `est_scores = lca_est[q, window]` where `window` is a slice around `q`.

Then, for each `i, j` (not counting `q`), we want to know how often the following occurs:
```
ref_scores[i] < ref_scores[j] => est_scores[i] < est_scores[j]
```
The slow algorithm for this is quadratic in the window size, since it looks at all pairs `i,j`.

This can be done more efficiently by the following method:

1. Sort `ref_scores`; let `idx` be the sorting index.
2. For each unique value `h` in `ref_scores`:
    a. Let `s, t` be the slice such that `ref_scores[s:t] == h`
    b. Find and sort the corresponding estimate scores: `pred[h] = sort(est_scores[idx][s:t])`
3. For each pair of reference score values (ie, levels to be compared), compute the number of inversions between `pred[h1], pred[h2]`: that is, values `a in pred[h1], b in pred[h2]` where `a >= b`.
4. Return the total number of detected inversions.




Running time
------------------

1. Step 1 takes `O(w log w)`
2. Step 2 in aggregate takes `O(w log w)` since each value is touched exactly once
3. Step 3 takes `O(H**2 w)`, where `H` is the number of levels in the reference. 
    - In the reduced measures, this simplifies to `O(H w)`, since each level is compared only to its immediate neighbors in the hierarchy.
    - The inversion detection is fast here because the inputs are in sorted order.  See [these notes](https://www.cs.princeton.edu/~wayne/kleinberg-tardos/pdf/05DivideAndConquerI.pdf) for a simple tutorial on inversion counting in totally ordered lists.

The normalization constant can be readily computed in `O(w)` time using the method described in the initial post of this thread.


Benchmark
---------------
The annotation spans a 30-second clip.  With a frame_size of 0.0125, this is equivalent to 4 minutes at frame_size=0.1.  There's probably still some room for improvement here, as this implementation has not been optimized to eliminate redundant calculations.

# New version
%timeit tmeasure(ref, est, window=None, frame_size=0.025, transitive=True)
1 loop, best of 3: 15.3 s per loop
```

```

I have two big-picture issues here, before I dive into the detailed code review:

1. I don't see why this should be handled in the lexer and not the parser. Doesn't the standard provide the exact grammar rules for atomic, which can then be incorporated into the parser? Note that atomic is making `t_ID` 2-3 times longer, which is a shame. Semantic inference should be done in the parser, unless there's a strong reason (context sensitivity) to do it otherwise.

2. pycparser doesn't do type checking and other validation of the AST. It will let you redefine types, variables and so on. So I don't think the extensive validity checking of Atomic, which requires you to modify the scope stack, is necessary. It significantly complicates the code. pycparser assumes it sees valid C code, and validation can be added as an AST pass after the AST is built, if needed.

####################################################################################################
## Adapted by GComyn on April 16, 2017
####################################################################################################

import logging
import re
import sys  # ## used for debug purposes
import time
import urllib2
import datetime

from base_adapter import BaseSiteAdapter, makeDate

from .. import exceptions as exceptions
from ..htmlcleanup import stripHTML

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

####################################################################################################
def getClass():
    return WWWWebNovelComAdapter


####################################################################################################
class WWWWebNovelComAdapter(BaseSiteAdapter):

    def __init__(self, config, url):
        BaseSiteAdapter.__init__(self, config, url)

        # 1252 is a superset of iso-8859-1. Most sites that claim to be iso-8859-1 (and some that
        # claim to be utf8) are really windows-1252.
        self.decode = ["utf8", "Windows-1252", "iso-8859-1"] 

        self.username = "NoneGiven" # if left empty, site doesn't return any message at all.
        self.password = ""
        self.is_adult = False

        # get storyId from url
        # https://www.webnovel.com/book/6831837102000205
        self.story.setMetadata('storyId',self.parsedUrl.path.split('/')[2])

        # Each adapter needs to have a unique site abbreviation.
        self.story.setMetadata('siteabbrev','wncom')

        # The date format will vary from site to site.
        # http://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#strftime-strptime-behavior
        ## There are no dates listed on this site, so am commenting this out
        #self.dateformat = "%Y-%b-%d"

####################################################################################################
    @staticmethod # must be @staticmethod, don't remove it.
    def getSiteDomain():
        # The site domain.  Does have www here, if it uses it.
        return 'www.webnovel.com'

####################################################################################################
    @classmethod
    def getSiteExampleURLs(cls):
        return "https://"+cls.getSiteDomain()+"/book/123456789012345"

####################################################################################################
    def getSiteURLPattern(self):
        return r"https://"+re.escape(self.getSiteDomain())+r"/book/*(?P<id>\d+)"

####################################################################################################
    ## Getting the chapter list and the meta data, plus 'is adult' checking.
    def doExtractChapterUrlsAndMetadata(self, get_cover=True):

        url = self.url

        try:
            data = self._fetchUrl(url)
        except urllib2.HTTPError, e:
            if e.code == 404:
                raise exceptions.StoryDoesNotExist('Error 404: {0}'.format(self.url))
            else:
                raise e

        if "We might have some troubles to find out this page." in data:
            raise exceptions.StoryDoesNotExist('{0} says: "" for url "{1}"'.format(self.getSiteDomain(),self.url))

        # use BeautifulSoup HTML parser to make everything easier to find.
        soup = self.make_soup(data)
        # removing all of the scripts
        for tag in soup.findAll('script') + soup.find_all('svg'):
            tag.extract()

        # Now go hunting for all the meta data and the chapter list.

        ## This is the block that holds the metadata
        bookdetails = soup.find('div', {'class':'g_col_8'})

        ## Title
        a = bookdetails.find('h2', {'class':'lh1d2'})
        self.story.setMetadata('title',stripHTML(a))
		
        # Find authorid and URL from... author url.
        paras = bookdetails.find_all('p')
        for para in paras:
            parat = stripHTML(para)
            if parat[:7] == 'Author:':
                self.story.setMetadata('author', parat.replace('Author:', '').strip())
                self.story.setMetadata('authorId', parat.replace('Author:', '').strip())
                ## There is no authorUrl for this site, so I'm setting it to the story url
                ## otherwise it defaults to the file location
                self.story.setMetadata('authorUrl', url)
            elif parat[:11] == 'Translator:':
                self.story.setMetadata('translator', parat.replace('Translator:', '').strip())
            elif parat[:7] == 'Editor:':
                self.story.setMetadata('editor', parat.replace('Editor:', '').strip())

        category = stripHTML(paras[0].strong).strip()
        self.story.setMetadata('category', category)

        ## Getting the ChapterUrls
        chaps = soup.find('div', {'id':'contentsModal'}).find_all('a')
        for chap in chaps:
            chap_title = stripHTML(chap).replace(': Blank Title', '').title()
            chap_Url = 'https:'+chap['href']
            self.chapterUrls.append((chap_title, chap_Url))
        
        self.story.setMetadata('numChapters', len(self.chapterUrls))

        if get_cover:
            cover_meta = soup.find('div', {'class':'g_col_4'}).find('img')
            cover_url = 'https:'+cover_meta['src']
            self.setCoverImage(url, cover_url)

        synopsis = soup.find('div', {'class':'det-abt'}).find('p')

        self.setDescription(url, synopsis)

        ## There are no published or updated dates listed on this site. I am arbitrarily setting
        ## these dates to the packaged date for now. If anyone else has an idea of how to get
        ## the original dates, please let me know [GComyn]
        ### I'd like to use the original date of the file, if this is an update, but I'm not proficient
        ### enough with programming to get it at this time. [GComyn]
        self.story.setMetadata('datePublished', makeDate(datetime.datetime.now().strftime ("%Y-%m-%d"), "%Y-%m-%d"))
        self.story.setMetadata('dateUpdated', makeDate(datetime.datetime.now().strftime ("%Y-%m-%d"), "%Y-%m-%d"))

        ## We don't know the status of the story from the index page, so we can't update it.

    # grab the text for an individual chapter.
    def getChapterText(self, url):
        logger.debug('Getting chapter text from: %s' % url)
		
        data = self._fetchUrl(url)
        html = self.make_soup(data)

        story = html.find('div', {'class':'cha-content'})
			
        if story == None:
            raise exceptions.FailedToDownload("Error downloading Chapter: %s!  Missing required element!" % url)

        for tag in story.find_all('form'):
            tag.extract()

        return self.utf8FromSoup(url,story)

I've been somewhat reluctant to implement the query property feature from alchy/Flask-SQLAlchemy in sqlservice.

You can actually get the same functionality as the query property using the SQLClient object itself:

```python
from sqlservice import SQLClient

from .models import Model

db = SQLClient(model_class=Model)

db.Role.filter_by(name=role).first()

# The above is the same as...
db.query(Role).filter_by(name=role).first()
```

The only limitation when compared to alchy's query property is that `db.<Model>` doesn't support custom query classes per model via the `<Model>.query_class` attribute.

I chose to implement the above instead of the query property b/c when you add the query property to a model class you are binding `Model.query` to the database session of `db`. I wanted to avoid that kind of coupling between models and the database session so that the models would be agnostic towards the session and not bound to any particular session if the user is using the same model classes across different sessions.

However, with that being said, implementing a query property on model classes is doable:

```python
from sqlalchemy import orm
from sqlservice import SQLClient

from .models import Model

class QueryProperty(object):
    def __init__(self, session, default_query_class):
        self.session = session
        self.default_query_class = default_query_class

    def __get__(self, model_instance, model_class):
        mapper = orm.class_mapper(model_class)

        if not mapper:
            return

        query_class = getattr(model_class, 'query_class', self.default_query_class)
        query_property = query_class(mapper, session=self.session)

        return query_property

class MySQLClient(SQLClient):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kargs)

        self.extend_model_with_query_property()

    def extend_model_with_query_property(self):
        self.model_class.query = QueryProperty(self.session, self.query_class)

db = MySQLClient(model_class=Model)
Role.query.filter_by(name=name).first()
```

NOTE: This is untested so there may be some issues with it. I've basically modified the `QueryProperty` class from [alchy.query.QueryProperty](https://github.com/dgilland/alchy/blob/f3d9a626da56c2b517576e8d18fb8a0bdba71e2a/alchy/query.py#L448) to better integrate with sqlservice but the implementation is roughly the same.

Hi @bekerov, here is a link to the documentation for this function for reference: [link](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/conversations.html#chatterbot.conversation.Statement.add_response). The `add_response` method adds the Response object as an element in the list of statements that the current statement is in response to. So, for example:

```python
from chatterbot.conversation import Statement, Response

statement = Statement("I am doing well.")
response = Response("Hello, how are you?")

statement.add_response(response)
```

Also, keep in mind that doing this only modifies the objects in memory, it does not commit them to the chat bot's database until you pass the statement object to the `update` method of the chat bot's storage adapter. Documentation on the available storage adapters can be found here: http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/storage/index.html

Let me know if you have any further questions.

Thanks for diving into this! A couple of thoughts:

There are three separate kinds of versions here (I apologize for not
naming them very well, I wasn't thinking of the future when I wrote that
code):

* "Application Versions", which are passed into the `create()`
  constructor via the `versions=` argument, come from the application,
  and are delivered to the application on the other end through the
  `w.get_versions()` API. This is a way for the two applications to
  negotiate their shared abilities.
* "Wormhole Versions", which are created by the wormhole library, sent
  through the encrypted channel in a phase=VERSION message, and are
  delivered to the wormhole library at the other end. They are not
  delivered to the application, but rather they're for the two wormholes
  to negotiate internal things like the upcoming "dilation" feature. The
  Application Versions are stored inside the Wormhole Versions
  dictionary, in a key named `app_versions`.
* the new "Client Version", which will be created by the wormhole
  library, included in the BIND message (not encrypted, at least not by
  the shared PAKE key), and delivered to the Mailbox Server. This is not
  seen by the remote side at all: it's consumed by the server.

We don't need to have the application pass in the Client Version; it
should be computed internally. So:

* let's leave cmd_send and cmd_receive alone, so no new arguments being
  passed into `create()`
* in `create()`, let's leave the `wormhole_versions` value alone
  (dictionary with a single `app_versions` key copied from the
  `versions=` argument)
* let's name the new field `client_version`, and make it a tuple of
  `(implementation, version)`, rather than two separate keys. So the
  current release would be `("python", "0.10.5")`
* let's use `from . import __version__` to pull in the version directly
* I admit that I'm not sure if Versioneer (which creates `_version.py`)
  provides bytes or strings, especially on py2-vs-3. You're absolutely
  right to prepare for either. But let's not switch on Python2-vs-3,
  let's just say that if `__version__` is bytes, treat it as UTF-8 and
  decode it (but tolerate problems, so use `errors="replace"`), and if
  it's str/unicode, leave it alone. That should work for both py2 and
  py3. Feel free to use something from the `six` module if that helps.
* pass the new string (always unicode at this point) into Boss with a
  new `client_version=` argument, instead of `wormhole_version`, and
  from the Boss into the RendezvousConnector
* deliver the new string in the BIND message as a single new argument
  named `client_version=` (all the keyword arguments get turned into
  JSON keys)
* let's leave `test_wormhole.py` alone: if applications want to pass
  non-strings into their `versions=` argument, that's their prerogative,
  the wormhole library should just deliver the value unmodified to the
  other application
* remove the extra `b. start()` change in `wormhole.py`

There is nothing in the software to guarantee that the sample rates are sorted, but in general, I would expect them to be sorted from analog slowest to analog fastest. (this implies digital fastest to digital slowest, but due to complex FPGA requirements, you will find frequent exceptions to this)
It's possible that analog sample rates are always sorted from fastest to slowest.

Basically, for any given performance setting (which directly relates to USB throughput) the available sample rates try to offer a trade off between digital and analog sample rates. (i.e. trade analog bandwidth for digital bandwidth, and vice versa)

I would update set_sample_rate_by_minimum to scan all performance options, and every sample rate option. I would ignore sorting and check every option, while tracking the lowest analog & digital pair that satisfies the requirement.

if you would like to select exactly the slowest sample rate for a given requirement, the USB bit rate can be calculated like this:

ADC width = 12 bits for Logic 8, Pro 8 and Pro 16.
ADC width = 8 bits for logic 4.

Bandwidth (bits per second) = (digital_sample_rate * digital_channel_count) + (analog_sample_rate * analog_channel_count * adc_width). 

That's the exact USB bandwidth requirement for any sample rate combination. There is no additional overhead. The lower the bandwidth, the higher the reliability for long duration captures. (this really only affects the original Logic and Logic 4, and only on poor-performance host controllers)

I wrote some code last week do simulate some survival time data. It could probably be improved but it's something
`import numpy as np 
import pandas as pd 

def data_generator(n=10000,seed=101):
    np.random.seed(seed=seed) #Setting seed
    df = pd.DataFrame(index=range(n)) #generating empty data frame with n rows
    df['eb'] = 1120176000 #Unix date time for start
    df['de'] = np.random.uniform(size=len(df))*450000000 #adding a random normal distribution
    df['dob'] = pd.to_datetime(df['eb'] + df['de'],unit='s').dt.date #converting to datetime
    df['z0'] = np.random.binomial(n=1,p=0.7,size=len(df)) #Simulating baseline confounder
    df['pa0'] = 1 / (1 + np.exp( -(np.log(1) - 4*0.7 + 4*df['z0']))) #Simulating probability for exposure
    df['a0'] = np.random.binomial(n=1,p=df['pa0'],size=len(df)) #Simulating binary exposure based on probability 
    df['predy'] = 18 + 10*df['a0'] - 9*df['z0'] + 0*df['a0']*df['z0'] #Simulating probability of outcome
    df['t'] = np.random.weibull(2,size=len(df)) * df['predy'] #Setting survival times from Weibull dist
    df['t2'] = np.where(df['t']>5,5,df['t']) #Administrative censoring time is 5
    df['delta'] = np.where(df['t']>5,0,1) #Event indicator if event happened before time 5
    return df 
`

@joshblum There are a couple options for this but nothing that doesn't change current expected functionality or isn't hacky. There is one option that isn't hacky but it requires a second hit to the DB. 

* Have `RegistrationManager.activate_user` return a tuple (e.g. `(profile_user, True)`). This would be similar to Django's `get_or_create` method but would require updating every call this this method (and would be a breaking change for end users that have custom registration views).
* Call the signal from within `RegistrationManager.activate_user` when `profile.activated` is false. Has side effects since this is called from places other than the view (and would need to pass is the `request` object).
* In `ActivationView.activate`, call the DB before `RegistrationManager.activate_user` to see if the profile was already activated.
* Delete the `RegistrationProfile` record when the user is activated or add `activated=False` to the query in `RegistrationManager.activate_user`. These will have the same side effects. 

It's not clear what the case is for passing back an already activated user. Ideally this wouldn't be necessary and if it isn't, then the last option is the best. If it is, then we'll need to decide which of the others to go for.

I'll work on supporting the draft sockets. You will need to make sure to compile pyzmq and libzmq yourself (avoid public wheels) because pyzmq binaries won't enable unstable draft APIs.

Instantiating the Queue attaches to the current (default) asyncio eventloop. Then, when you later start the zmq loop, a new eventloop is created and installed, but the Queue is still attached to the first eventloop, which never runs.

To work with an alternate eventloop, the eventloop must be installed prior to anything registering listeners. Calling `install()` before instantiating `Queue()` should do it:

```python
import asyncio

# install the zmq asyncio loop before anything
# registers listeners (e.g. creating a Queue)
import zmq.asyncio
zmq.asyncio.install()

queue = asyncio.Queue()

async def pushing():
    while True:
        await queue.put("hello")
        await asyncio.sleep(1)

async def pulling():
    while True:
        greeting = await queue.get()
        print(greeting)

def zmq_async_loop():
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    print(loop)
    tasks = [
        pushing(),
        pulling()
    ]
    loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.wait(tasks))
    loop.close()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    zmq_async_loop()
```

Some of the MLST databases have poor quality data in them and ARIBA warns you about this.  The first warning message indicates identical allele profiles have different STs (which should never happen). In the second case it is warning about a very long allele sequence compared to the rest. Normally the length of the allele sequences is very similar, and to have one thats way outside of that can indicate poor quality data (its very rare that its real). With some databases where manual curation is lax, this occurs quite a bit (e.g. sequences which dont translate to proteins, truncated).

There seem to be at least two separate issues in this thread (and both might be different from the OP's issue...).
For the examples provided by @ldesegur  and @edjubuh, the problem is that `jsonpickle` does not automatically handle nested class definitions, the class has to be given explicitly as an argument to `classes` when decoding. For the example that @kapilkd13 provided, the problem is that `jsonpickle` does not correctly handle builtin functions.

Here's another test case that shows the root of the first problem:
```Python
import jsonpickle

class Outer(object):
    class Inner(object):
        pass

print(jsonpickle.encode(Outer.Inner))
```
The output is `{"py/type": "__main__.Inner"}>`, showing that `jsonpickle` did not correctly encode the class name. This is because the `importable_name` function in `jsonpickle.util` assembles the name from the module name (`__main__`) and the class name (`Inner`), which does not include the name of the outer class. This is easily fixable for Python >=3.3 which provides an attribute `__qualname__` to get the fully qualified name, but unfortunately needs complex codes that inspects the code file (see e.g. https://github.com/wbolster/qualname). For Python >=3.3, this is the necessary change:
```diff
diff --git a/jsonpickle/util.py b/jsonpickle/util.py
index 8566450..dff7dac 100644
--- a/jsonpickle/util.py
+++ b/jsonpickle/util.py
@@ -507,7 +507,7 @@ def importable_name(cls):
     True
 
     """
-    name = cls.__name__
+    name = cls.__qualname__
     module = translate_module_name(cls.__module__)
     return '%s.%s' % (module, name)
```
`print(jsonpickle.encode(Outer.Inner))` then correctly prints `{"py/type": "__main__.Outer.Inner"}`.

However, this does not fix the decoding, because the decoding code assumes that only the last part of the "importable name" is a class name, and the rest is a module; in this case, it would therefore try to import the `Inner` class from the `__main__.Outer` module which obviously fails.

It might be cleaner to store the module and class information separately in a tuple, but the following change makes it simply try all options:
```diff
diff --git a/jsonpickle/unpickler.py b/jsonpickle/unpickler.py
index 283d54b..06864e9 100644
--- a/jsonpickle/unpickler.py
+++ b/jsonpickle/unpickler.py
@@ -606,13 +606,21 @@ def loadclass(module_and_name, classes=None):
         except KeyError:
             pass
     # Otherwise, load classes from globally-accessible imports
-    try:
-        module, name = module_and_name.rsplit('.', 1)
-        module = util.untranslate_module_name(module)
-        __import__(module)
-        return getattr(sys.modules[module], name)
-    except (AttributeError, ImportError, ValueError):
-        return None
+    names = module_and_name.split('.')
+    # First assume that everything up to the last dot is the module name,
+    # then try other splits to handle classes that are defined within
+    # classes
+    for up_to in range(len(names)-1, 0, -1):
+        module = util.untranslate_module_name('.'.join(names[:up_to]))
+        try:
+            __import__(module)
+            obj = sys.modules[module]
+            for class_name in names[up_to:]:
+                obj = getattr(obj, class_name)
+            return obj
+        except (AttributeError, ImportError, ValueError) as ex:
+            continue
+    return None
```
With these changes, my example provided above and the examples provided by @ldesegur and @edjubuh work fine.

For the second issue, the problem is relatively obvious, the output of this code:
```Python
import jsonpickle

print(jsonpickle.encode(dir))
```
is `{"py/object": "__builtin__.builtin_function_or_method"}` which is of course impossible to convert back to the `dir` function.

Here are the changes that seem to fix this issue:
```diff
diff --git a/jsonpickle/util.py b/jsonpickle/util.py
index dff7dac..ed6bd8d 100644
--- a/jsonpickle/util.py
+++ b/jsonpickle/util.py
@@ -110,7 +110,8 @@ def is_object(obj):
     False
     """
     return (isinstance(obj, object) and
-            not isinstance(obj, (type, types.FunctionType)))
+            not isinstance(obj, (type, types.FunctionType,
+                                 types.BuiltinFunctionType)))
 
 
 def is_primitive(obj):
@@ -277,7 +278,7 @@ def is_module_function(obj):
     """
 
     return (hasattr(obj, '__class__') and
-            isinstance(obj, types.FunctionType) and
+            isinstance(obj, (types.FunctionType, types.BuiltinFunctionType)) and
             hasattr(obj, '__module__') and
             hasattr(obj, '__name__') and
             obj.__name__ != '<lambda>')

For example the `Upload` task takes `url` as a parameter, but in a downstream task `UploadToFoo` I don't want the user to be able to change the value of `url` from the command line -- maybe it always needs to be `foo.com`, or else the implementation of `UploadToFoo` doesn't make sense. Switching the name to multiGMPE makes sense, so this is now fixed

For most use cases the input to the MultiGMPE is a string indicating the mapping of the GMPEs to the IMTs. Something like:
`{PGA: BooreEtAl2014, SA(0.5): BindiEtAl2014Rjb}`

For the case of the GMPETable, this would work like:
`{PGA: BooreEtAl2014, SA(1.0): GMPETable(gmpe_table=path/to/table.hdf5)}`
a test case for this is added.

For the XML, so far in prototyping this has been attached to a particular geotechnical calculator, and since that needs it's own logic tree then it takes the form as below, with the GMPE being instantiated from the string inside the `uncertaintyModel` text:
```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>

<nrml xmlns:gml="http://www.opengis.net/gml"
      xmlns="http://openquake.org/xmlns/nrml/0.4">
    <logicTree logicTreeID='lt1'>
        <logicTreeBranchingLevel branchingLevelID="bl1">
            <logicTreeBranchSet uncertaintyType="gmpeModel" branchSetID="bs1"
                    applyToTectonicRegionType="Active Shallow Crust">
                <logicTreeBranch branchID="b11">
                    <uncertaintyModel>{PGA: BooreEtAl2014, SA(1.0): AkkarEtAlRjb2014}</uncertaintyModel>
                    <gdemModel>HAZUSLiquefaction</gdemModel>
                    <uncertaintyWeight>0.5</uncertaintyWeight>
                </logicTreeBranch>
                <logicTreeBranch branchID="b12">
                    <uncertaintyModel>{PGA: BindiEtAl2014Rjb, SA(1.0): AkkarBommer2010}</uncertaintyModel>
                    <gdemModel>HAZUSLiquefaction</gdemModel>
                    <uncertaintyWeight>0.5</uncertaintyWeight>
                </logicTreeBranch>
            </logicTreeBranchSet>
        </logicTreeBranchingLevel>
    </logicTree>
</nrml>
```
This has its own logic tree reader (coming in a future PR), which is a subclass of the GSIM Logic Tree with some minor modifications. This is simply because a given geotechnical hazard calculation branch has to combine both the choice of calculator and the choice of models. This could be replaced by having a separate logic tree for the GSIMs and a separate logic tree for the choice of geotechnical calculator, but this requires a substantial change of the logic tree calculators in general given that certain geotechnical calculators will require multiple IMTs. Setting a given branch and associating it to its given GMPE-IMT set in this manner would meet all of the use cases that we have encountered so far in practice (which, admittedly, is not many)

@for %%F in ("%cd%") do @set "folder=%%~nxF"

OK, I think I've found the root of the issue; https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/pipenv/utils.py#L538 calls `shellquote` on `sys.executable`, which wraps it in quotes.

This then gets passed to HackedPythonVersion, which is how it ends up being used further down the stack.

Just had a quick look at `dateutil`'s source code, and I suppose you're able to patch it like this:
```
>>> import dateutil.parser
>>> dateutil.parser.parse('65-12-01')
datetime.datetime(2065, 12, 1, 0, 0)
>>> old_init = dateutil.parser._parser._ymd.__init__
>>> def new_init(self, *args, **kwargs):
...   old_init(self, *args, **kwargs)
...   self.century_specified = True
... 
>>> dateutil.parser._parser._ymd.__init__ = new_init
>>> dateutil.parser.parse('65-12-01')
datetime.datetime(65, 12, 1, 0, 0)
>>> 
```
So the parsed datetime object won't have the year component modified. You may later check the year value and apply the appropriate operation.

Thanks for the PR, but the solution was actually a bit more complex than this (because we need to support Python 2 and 3).  You can see the changes I made [here](https://github.com/Parsely/streamparse/compare/bb937d3024a8ac02fa9d42b64332e4893e9a2d4c...aa5bb7e).  I'll push out a bugfix release shortly.

I would really like to add support for using PEX files instead of virtualenvs to make supporting exactly this use case simpler (see #212), but I haven't had the time to do that yet. Also, since you pointed out that JARs don't preserve executable permissions, that is going to be tricky to do.

You are correct that `command` cannot contain spaces, which is why we use `streamparse_run` instead of `python -m streamparse.run`.  You can see my complaints about that in the commit message for 3d2760ace8e9156399ba17a5ec57e1f579eed221.

> Since all our code is self-contained and dockerized we can not and do not want to rely on virtualenv and ssh

What other people in your situation usually do is have the virtualenv get setup somewhere inside their container as part of their Dockerfile, and then have `install_virtualenv` and `use_ssh_for_nimbus` both set to false as described [here](http://streamparse.readthedocs.io/en/stable/quickstart.html#disabling-configuring-virtualenv-creation).

An alternative is to also set `use_virtualenv` to false and just install all your requirements using `/usr/local/bin/pip` so that everything is available without activating a virtualenv at all.  That only is recommended if you've got a single container per topology, so you never share that the same python executable across topologies.

> The problems with this were reported by @rhyolight when installation went twice through installing numpy, as different versions were required for core(bindings) and nupic.

See: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/2607

This was actually because nupic.bindings and nupic required different versions of numpy (one was `==1.9.2` and the other was `>=1.9.2`, so because the `>=` requirement was fulfilled first and the latest release was 1.9.3, it got installed. Then during the nupic build, 1.9.2 was installed. 

After fixing both requirements to have the same explicit dependency, this no longer happens. But thank you for following up on the video with this report!

> ValueError: can't delete _p_oid of cached object

That's expected in both cache implementations when used with their corresponding persistent type ([C](https://github.com/zopefoundation/persistent/blame/master/persistent/cPersistence.c#L1162) and [Python](https://github.com/zopefoundation/persistent/blob/master/persistent/persistence.py#L125)) But when the C persistent object is put in the pure-Python PickleCache, as happened when the import was wrong, the C-level object didn't know that it was in the Python-level cache, and so the check didn't get done. Now that the imports and implementations match, the behaviour should be consistent, and the `if` side of that branch can probably be dropped.

@njsmith Ok I dug into this a bit... 

I think the issue is that 'f16' is the dtype for `np.float128`. The computer I ran this on--at the time--had `np.float128` available, but my Windows Server 2016 computer does not have that dtype available. (I'm a little confused because I can't seem to figure out what I did that got the `float128` datatype to show up in the first place... Both are 64 bit systems... one is a laptop, one is a xeon e5 virtual machine). 

patsy tries to promote the data to a "wider" dtype [here](https://github.com/pydata/patsy/blob/master/patsy/util.py#L294). So if you make the pickle on a computer with `np.float128`, then try to load it on a computer without that available, I think this will happen. 

Options:
* Maybe only promote up to `np.float64`. 
* Have an error handle that says when you load a datatype that doesn't exist on the current environment, to gently fail. 
* re-write tests to not have this issue

Thoughts?

You can pass your own Python Requests Session for (most) DataReader

see http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#proxies
and http://stackoverflow.com/questions/30837839/how-can-i-set-a-single-proxy-for-a-requests-session-object


```python
import requests
proxies = {
    'http': 'http://cache.domain.com:3128'
}
session = requests.Session()
session.proxies.update(proxies)

import pandas_datareader as pdr
f = pdr.DataReader("F", "yahoo", "2016-01-01", "2016-12-31", session=session)
```

Hi @albertvaka 

> How can I recover from this scenario? Is there any way I can ask it to sync everything from scratch, instead of using WALs?

Reinitialize the replica with `patronictl reinit`. This is exactly "sync everything from scratch"

In order to avoid WAL files not yet recovered by a replica to be recycled on the primary consider enabling replication slots (http://patroni.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dynamic_configuration.html) in Patroni. This is normally a default, so I am wondering whether you changed it manually or are running PostgreSQL 9.3, which doesn't support replication slots? In the latter case you need to establish WAL archiving (which is always a good idea) in order to avoid replicas falling out of date using just the steaming replication.

Seems to be there are only 2 differences between the failover and the switchover:

- failover always requires the candidate
- the check for failing over to the synchronous replica is not enforced during the failover if the cluster is running in a synchronous mode.

Otherwise, switchover seems to be just an alias of the failover.

In `patronictl`, there is a check of whether the cluster to switchover has a master, but that check is not duplicated in the API itself, allowing to switchover in the cluster without the master via the REST API, potentially loosing data. I think this check should be present in the API itself.

    def get_overridden_settings(cls, worker_index: int = 0, db_prefix: str = 'test_'):
        # This method forms a dictionary of settings to override for test. 
        # In my project CLICKHOUSE_DB is set in settings, so I have to create Database object here. 
        # But I don't want to create database here, as this method may be used for other purposes
        # (for example, to clean up all test databases. It would be strange to create db in order to drop it)
        return {} # some code here

    @classmethod
    def get_databases(cls, worker_index: int = 0, db_prefix: str = 'test_'):
        # Now I have single ClickHouse database. 
        # But it may be changed in future, and method returns multiple database descriptors to pass to other methods.  
        # It may also be used in procedures that doesn't need db creation.
        cfg = cls.get_overridden_settings(worker_index=worker_index, db_prefix=db_prefix)
        return [cfg['CLICKHOUSE_DB']]

    @classmethod
    def migrate_database(cls, db)
       # This method creates database if needed and migrates it by db objects returned by get_databases()
       # db for this TestCase is infi.clickhouse_orm.Database() object.
       db.create_database() 
       # some migrate code here
```

The difference between `_dynet` and `dynet` is a leftover from when we had different modules for GPU and CPU (`_dynet` and `_gdynet`), and then dynet.py used to choose the correct one. Importing `_dynet` was never intended as something a user should do.

Another main difference is that `dynet` calls Init() as part of the loading process. I think this is more natural than requiring the use to call `Init()` by themselves each time they use dynet.

I suggest we remove `Init()`, `Init_from_params` and `DynetParams` from the python interface altogether, and allow only the `dynet_config` version.

Some things that are currently set in `Init`, like the random seed, or auto-batching, we can also allow to do post-init, by slightly adapting the C++ version.

Can you describe the behavior you expect from PyCharm? What is your personal use case? (Is it _Run Configurations_?)

Proprietary PyCharm helpers
-------------------------------

For what I can see, in PyCharm 2016.3.1 you can create a _Run Configuration_ of type _Behave_. PyCharm uses its own set of helper scripts to execute those, which rely on an installed `behave` module and probably on its default execution pattern:
``` python
/home/peter/.virtualenvs/painless-website/bin/python /opt/pycharm-2016.3/helpers/pycharm/behave_runner.py
Testing started at 23:54 ...
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/pycharm-2016.3/helpers/pycharm/behave_runner.py", line 16, in <module>
    from behave.formatter.base import Formatter
ImportError: No module named 'behave'
```
With `behave` installed locally the output of a _Behave_ run configuration looks more like:
``` python
/home/peter/.virtualenvs/python-testing/bin/python /opt/pycharm-2016.3/helpers/pycharm/behave_runner.py
Testing started at 23:58 ...

Process finished with exit code 0
```
In other words, PyCharm relies on the "fact" that _behave_ is run via the `behave` shell command. However, with _behave-django_ we run a Django _management_ command to launch _behave_, which is how we integrate into Django and launch the `LiveServerTestCase`:
``` bash
$ python manage.py behave
```
I'm not quite sure what JetBrains would have to change to make this all work with `behave-django`. Simply running the management command from PyCharm may be one (part of the) solution.

Integration
-----------

Access to the [`base_url` or, better, to `get_url()`](https://github.com/behave/behave-django/blob/master/docs/usage.rst#web-browser-automation) only depends on running the management command. Those properties are attached dynamically by our code to the `context` variable _behave_ provides during a test run. They expose properties available from a Django `LiveServerTestCase` to the BDD test run.

On linux the socket will remain closed for a while, to prevent stale messages from be sent to a wrong server.  In this case we want to serve new requests immediately.

See
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/10705680/how-can-i-restart-a-basehttpserver-instance/10706603#10706603


Fix is below,  I added a returning of the http server so it can be closed cleanly by the main loop
Something like:
if __name__ == '__main__':
    port=sys.argv[1]
    # Start up the server to expose the metrics.
    server=start_http_server(int(port))
    try:
        process_commands()
    finally:
        server.shutdown()
        server.close()

Here's a simple idea that I've used a few times, maybe it will help out here. 

```
import os
import threading
import sys

def log_for_docker(generator, line_prefix='', print_empty=False):
  '''
  To get generator, use `client.logs(container=cid, stream=True)`
  It's perfectly reasonable to use this method once for STDOUT and 
  once for STDERR 
  '''
  def log_docker(generator, print_empty, line_prefix):
    for line in generator:
      if print_empty or line.strip():
        sys.stdout.write(line_prefix)
        sys.stdout.write(line)
  r_log = threading.Thread(target=log_docker, args=(generator,print_empty, line_prefix))
  r_log.daemon = True
  r_log.start()
```

Naturally this might cause output to be mixed when using multiple threads, but I've yet to have such serious issues that I've cared. If someone wants to modify this to take an output mutex, they could easily make it such that only one background thread is printing at a single time without hurting the performance of the main thread of execution. That's no guarantee that the main thread and the currently-printing background thread won't write at the same time, it would just help if you've got many container logs being tracked in one application

I believe this is probably SQL Server behaviour, rather than a driver quirk.  In SQL Server, tables prefixed with "#" are visible only within a session.  When the session expires, the table is dropped.  Tables prefixed with "##" are visible across sessions.  When SQL Server executes a parameterised query, SQL Server wraps that query within a session.  Hence when that query completes, the session expires, and any "#" tables expire with it.  As follows:
```python
import pyodbc
conn = pyodbc.connect('...', autocommit=True)
cursor = conn.cursor()

cursor.execute("SELECT CAST(1 as int) AS A INTO #t1")  # succeeds
cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM #t1").fetchall()  # this query succeeds

cursor.execute("SELECT CAST(? as int) AS A INTO #t2", (1,))  # apparently succeeds
cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM #t2").fetchall()  # fails with: pyodbc.ProgrammingError: ('42S02', "[42S02] [Microsoft][SQL Server Native Client 10.0][SQL Server]Invalid object name '#t2'. (208) (SQLExecDirectW)")
```
Table #t2 is dropped as soon as the query completes, hence the last query fails.

Also, the path after /D does not support ", i.e. quotes. It also needs to be at the end of the parameter list because all parameters will be appended to the path otherwise. A working option string is therefore

Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86_64.exe /AddToPath=1 /RegisterPython=1 /InstallationType=AllUsers /S /D=C:\my_directory

You probably want to install for `Just Me` and also do not allow the installer to add to `PATH`.

To get a newer conda, you could install the latest Minconda instead, then if you want all of Anaconda you can use `conda install anaconda`.

I recently experimented with this. I believe this is related to the way Vivado tools work. When you have an IP core that is part of a hierarchy, you can only generate simulation files for the whole hierarchy starting at the top. For example, if you are using IP integrator and you have a block diagram (top.bd) which has an AXI interconnect, and a few peripherals, you can only call `generate_target simulation` on the top level block diagram (top.bd), but not on any of the individual components.

If, on the other hand, you have a IP project where IP/cores are generated as separate entities, I believe you should be able to use the exact method that it outlined in the add_vivado_ip example.

So my suggestions:

- Use an IP Project where IP are generated independently (not as a hierarchy) and use the flow as shown in the example, or
- If using IP integrator, modify the example given so that you can specify a block diagram to have simulation sources generated and the compilation order returned.

Hi @nitzmahone 
I thoughts about this one, and I think this is confusing and you're right. I will release soon the new major version (kwargs only args, new LRO implementation, etc.) and I want to take this opportunity to refactor this one as well.

I want to:
- Make `api_version` and `profile` mutually exclusive. This solves this issue here. Profile can take a `None` key if you want to say "everything else I didn't define explicitly".
- I'm adding a `Profile` class. By default the new signature will then be:
```python
def __init__(self, credentials, subscription_id, api_version=None, base_url=None, profile=Profile.default)
```
And `Profile.default` will be by default valued `Profile.latest`.

Meaning you can create your client this way if you want to explictly say "I want latest":
```python
client = ComputeMangementClient(creds, sub, profile=Profile.latest)
```
I will support the known profiles as well:
```python
client = ComputeMangementClient(creds, sub, profile=Profile.v2017_09_03)
```
`profile`can still get a dictionnary as right now, I don't change that.

If you use `default`, you could also change the default profile as the application level:
```python
Profile.default = Profile.v2017_09_03
```
This would mutate all created client automatically to the new profile if you do that in the middle of your application. 

I would really appreciate if you can give me your thoughts on this :)
Thanks!

I would love to get this branch merged, so I can stop using a custom build of schematics.  I re-read the thread, and after some consideration I still like my original proposal for its simplicity.  

Let me summarize what I think are the key points of this PR:

- Use cases would be anything that should be deferred until model instantiation for performance or timing reasons, like checking a database or file system for a list of registered options.

That's a great point! Those ops are just wrapped as primitives, and so there's no problem on the forward pass. However, they also don't lead to errors on the backward pass, and I think we want those errors:

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad

print grad(lambda x: x > 5)(10.)  # prints 0 and the 'independent of input' warning
```

Hmm...

+1 for the rule that the returned grad always has the same shape as the value passed in for the argument with respect to which you're differentiating.

(By the way, your example code has `import numpy as np` at the top, but for autograd to work correctly with numpy functions you need to use `import autograd.numpy as np` instead. That's not an issue for your example code, though.)

In cases like your example code, one solution would be to broadcast manually before you evaluate the function, rather than leaving the broadcasting to the `+` inside the function:

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad

f = lambda x,y: (x+y)**2
fgrad = grad(f, 0)

x = np.linspace(0, 1, 11)
y = 0.0

x, y = np.broadcast_arrays(x, y)
```

Then `fgrad(x, y)` has the same value as `fgrad(y, x)`.

They verify you actually own the domain. Hence, `Domain Validated` certs.

@joohoi: having talked to @olabini and read the nginx code a bit more closely, I do think there are circumstances where vhost creation can make sense.  Those are essentially when vhosts for a new (to nginx) domain will be created on a transient basis and then reverted/deleted after a cert has been obtained.  That can make sense if the nginx plugin is used with `certonly`, or if it is used with a different plugin (like a future mail server plugin) as the installer.  Such vhosts can be sensibly created during `perform`, so long as they are deleted during `cleanup`.

I do think that supporting this case is lower than Nginx MVP priority, and there's an argument for possibly even requiring an extra flag for it `--nginx-novel-domains` or something, since at least some of the time this case will arise because the admin is confused, not because they want to obtain a cert for a domain they don't intend to have nginx answer for.

@potuz This does not at all seem to be a bug. If you can show me a valid `RRULE` that doesn't parse correctly, then we can work from there, but it seems to me that your issue is that something you're using is generating `RRULE` objects that set a start date of `DATE-TIME`. Silently ignoring a malformed `RRULE` is not the right behavior (and hard-coding a specific value to ignore is *definitely* not the right thing to do).

Consider that `DATE-TIME` doesn't actually represent a datetime, so what exactly is it supposed to be set as?

If you can show me somewhere in [the iCalendar RFC](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2445) that `DATE-TIME` is a valid value for `DTSTART` (and what exactly it means), I'd be happy to implement the specified behavior. I'll leave this open for a bit to allow for comment.

It is not really valid to assume specific encoding of the paths: in linux file name is absolutely any sequence of bytes which does not include `/` (for it being a path separator) and NUL (for it terminating a C string). Command arguments are only not allowed to have NUL, but otherwise lack any encoding requirements. According to Python documentation, [os.path.ismount](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.ismount) must accept any path-like object, and “path-like” does [include](https://docs.python.org/3/glossary.html#term-path-like-object) `bytes`. Same goes for `Popen`’s cwd. And though I don’t find any details of what kind of strings it accepts as an arguments, I do not know any code which will call that with non-unicode (non-str in Python-3\*) strings.

I don't think we need multi-transactional analysis for the contract reported by suhabe. 

The thing we want to discover is whether it is possible to write 0 to param. So we basically want to discover invariants, and to do that we shouldn't need multiple transaction runs. We would, however, need to model the constructor.

How this situation could be solved:
Execute the constructior and constrain storage using that analysis. From that we would get the constraint param > 0.
Symbolically execute the program to find any writes to param, and use their constraints to determine invariance of param. (Not necessary in this case)
In the assertion check use invariants/constraints discovered to see reachability of revert state

Note however, that the default table representation in nuZ is based on sparse hash-tables. 
We don't have BDD based tables. The closest is a table representation based on ternary bit-vectors, which is used for network dataplane analysis in some applications, but generally it is not going to be suitable for datalog programs extracted from circuit-like scenarios.
So for this example, disable the unbound compression as illustrated above and it works, but if the set of reachable tuples explodes, the hash-table representation will not be adequate.

> It makes me feel that the logic (making statement know arch, which is something it should have known already after creation) is unnecessarily "leaked" to angr from PyVEX, or to any other project that uses it.

Yeah, I wavered on that myself. But I felt the cleanliness combined with following libVEX's example and still supporting pretty-printing of statements and expressions at the `IRSB` level were convincing reasons to make the change. How often is it that angr users or devs print lifted statements and expressions without printing the entire block? It's not often in my work, but maybe it is in yours and other's.

For general use of PyVEX, the user wouldn't expect to be required to include architecture information when libVEX doesn't require it. For all use cases other than printing out lifted code (granted, that's angr's main use case, for now), requiring extra info like `arch` is annoying.

> I might be wrong on this point, but for register name pretty-printing, tyenv (or the result_size) is not useful. The offset itself is enough to pinpoint a specific register.

Yes, having only the offset is enough to get a register name. But it would be the name used to refer the full-sized register. E.g., `rax` vs `eax`, `ax`, etc.

After looking at the code the ```EmptyGangaObject``` class is never used anymore I think over creating an _empty_ instance of the class which has been stored in the Registry.
(An ```EmtpyGangaObject``` used to be created which was then _mocked_ up to look like the object in question which caused some level of problems due to member variables and such not being fully initialized in all cases).
As such it's not possible to easily disallow the object from being marked dirty without storing knowledge of whether the object has been fully loaded from disk within the object.

Ultimately I'd like to redesign all of this store a ```_inMemory``` boolean in each GangaObject.
This would default to True when an object is created in memory and is set to false when an _empty_ instance is created by the Registry.
This would mean the ```__get__``` method can more cleanly ask for a job to be loaded if required rather than by inspecting the object itself (which is currently done).

(I'm much more of a fan of moving to make explicit calls to the Registry to ```load``` an object from the Registry if needed rather than this current mechanism of ```_getReadAccess``` and ```_getWriteAccess``` which confuses the matter somewhat. I'm not sure if the read/write distinction was important in the past but due to ```SessionLock``` I'm not sure we can properly have _read_ access to the same job from multiple ganga sessions.)

Well, there were certainly a lot of dead ends! My eventual path started with the information from the reported issue that binary was somehow getting through. Tracing the request object from the entry point, I found that all requests come in with a binary `request.body` but, depending on `Content-Type`, either `request.body` or `request.json_body` is used:

https://github.com/Cornices/cornice/blob/ded8422de830688ca5d2dd1656feddce331b4b20/cornice/validators/__init__.py#L52-L58

So my hypothesis was that the `is_json` regex was not matching, and `body` was simply getting assigned from the binary `request.body`. At that point I could reproduce the original error message with a request using `Content-Type:text/plain`.

Then it was just a matter of seeing how other routes (e.g., `/buckets`) handled content-types other than application/json and reproduce that on `/batch`!

If you are enabling Infrared and Infrared2 on the R200 in your application, this would be the first thing to disable. However, it might be that your target platform simply struggles to handle two 1080P streams + depth data. What is the CPU utilization when the problem is happening? When you are requesting RGB data from librealsense, we are internally doing YUY to RGB conversion, and it is known to be very CPU intensive. Even if SSE instructions are available and enabled, this might be too much for some hosts to handle and keep up with the USB transfer. 
Could you try your test, but ask for YUY format instead of RGB in the sample App? (You could consider offloading YUY to RGB conversion to the GPU. We are not doing any of that inside librealsense to avoid adding OpenCL as a dependency, but I know it has been done before)

Hi all,

There are a couple of issues mixed in here, so let me try to address as much as I can:

>> occasionally I want to calc_locs to be more specific for the elastic workflow as well. Maybe PassCalcLocs should be refactored in a way that makes it possible to get calc locs for a specific firework, e. g. rather than pushing the calc_locs into a list, set a specific key or something. 
>> It's my understanding that calc_loc = True will grab the location of the most recently completed calculation (rather than the parent) so I cannot use it for this situation.

The calc_locs has two ways of getting previous calculation data via the CopyVaspOutputs Firetask:
(i) For calc_loc=True, it will look through the array of data in the "calc_locs" key in the fw_spec and give you back the last array entry. What this means practically is that if you have a single parent, calc_loc=True should will copy from the location of that single parent. If you have multiple parents, calc_loc=True will give you the location of the parent that completed most recently. Note that if you have some side-branch of calculations that finished more recently than your parent, you won't get information from that side branch. @blondegeek , does this resolve your concern?
(ii) For calc_loc = <some string> (rather than True), you will grab a specific calculation from the history of calculations. Note that all entries in the calc_locs key have a "name" parameter. This name parameter can be easily set when using the PassCalcLocs Firetask; the default used in most Firework implementations is the name of the Firework. But you can easily code a Firework that sets the name of entry in the calc_locs key to "structure A" or "Bob the calculation" in PassCalcLocs. Then, for the downstream CopyVaspOutputs firetasks (anything downstream), if you set calc_loc="Bob the calculation", it will keep going back in history until it encounters Bob. So this allows you to pick out a specific entry from the calc_locs.

>>  If so, what's the best work around? Creating a new class that inherits from StaticFW that has this parameter?

For @blondegeek , it's possible that the default implementation is just fine. Let me know if not.
For @montoyjh, the StaticFW currently doesn't let you use mode (ii) if grabbing a specific calc_loc by the name. This is easy enough to fix though. I'll do it now.

>> Also worth noting that I think we might refactor a lot of the PassX FWs that have proliferated, seems like they could probably fall under some more general structure.

Let's look into this separately, e.g. in a separate issue ticket or something. I am slowly making my way through reviewing all the atomate packages (as you may have noticed).

Follow-up of a concern I had while discussing the PR referenced in this issue (#64):
* https://github.com/opentracing/opentracing-python/pull/64#discussion_r165644789
* https://github.com/opentracing/opentracing-python/pull/64#discussion_r166242076

After thinking about it and taking in consideration use cases we have, I would like to continue the discussion here about the current implementation of `start_active_span` ([code](https://github.com/opentracing/opentracing-python/blob/90fea0bb4612584acdfbfc34fa70f46a8ba348a0/opentracing/tracer.py#L54-L100)). In general, I think we should not expose an advanced functionality (closing or not closing a `Scope`) to end-users (developers), considering a wrong usage of this functionality will definitely lead to mistakes and wrong/missing/hard to debug traces.

I would prefer to expose the functionality for Tracer implementers (or `contrib` modules where we can make it explicit all the time) and to end-users with specific edge cases (i.e. using callbacks, asyncio loop with detached executions etc...).

Said that, if my first point makes sense, we should consider having a default value so that we cover most of the cases (optional parameter). Then we may have two questions:
1. What about the Java API that doesn't have a default?
2. What is the default?

My opinions:
1. I think here we're talking about implementations and not specifications. As we've already discussed during our last cross-language call, I think it's good enough in having a different behavior because Python (and Python use cases) are different from Java. I would say +1 in having a default value here on Python.
2. Since how most of the Python applications are designed, it should be very likely that end-users want to close a `Span` after closing the `Scope`. In some cases (and not all of them) where callbacks and coroutines are used, changing the behavior is still allowed. Giving our feedback most of our users never had this requirement, so a default of `True` is what I would advocate.

Hi @nguyenhoan1988,
As of now, a potential solution is to learn unsupervised word vectors on the unlabeled tweets (e.g. using `./fasttext skipgram`) and then use these vectors to initialize the classifier, using the `-pretrainedVectors` command line option. This option is used to specify a text file containing pre-trained word vectors (e.g. the `.vec` file outputed by `./fasttext skipgram`).

```

I've had a chance to look a little more closely at django-ses and django-amazon-ses, and can see how it would be useful to also add SES support to django-anymail. 

If you're just sending email, switching from Anymail to another EMAIL_BACKEND should be pretty straightforward. (That's the whole point of Django's EmailBackend abstraction.) 

But if you're also handling email tracking webhook events (bounces, etc.), then it'd be helpful to have normalized SES events so you can reuse your existing Anymail signal handlers. (django-ses supports SES events, but defines its own signal API which is, naturally, somewhat different from Anymail's.)

On the other hand, django-ses supports some features that aren't likely to show up in django-anymail (like an admin reporting panel, and a sleep-based delay to throttle sends). The packages have different goals, and there's plenty of room for them to coexist.

I'd welcome a PR for SES support in Anymail. (My own focus is on finishing inbound support first.) The PR doesn't have to be fully baked—work-in-progress is fine, and probably better for something like adding a new ESP.

I would suggest basing it on boto3 (like django-amazon-ses) rather than boto2 (like django-ses). The two botos can run side by side, and I believe boto3 is preferred for new code.

$ pulumi login https://pulumi.acmecorp.com

You guys probably have this figured out, but here's my $0.02.

I like that `ray.remote` is a decorator, and the common usage has very clean and easy syntax. But the best way to really think about it is as a class factory, returning a new class. For fancy stuff, one should also keep the original class:

1. As we already know, you can inherit from a class intended as an actor. You can do:
    ```python
    class B(object):
        pass
    
    @ray.remote
    class B_actor(B):
        pass
    
    class C(B):
        pass
    ```
    Or more elegantly `B_actor = ray.remote(B)`.
    Any dynamic reference to `B`, including inside its own body, should be bound statically in `B_actor` to the value it had when `ray.remote` was called.
    You should only replace `B` with its remote version once you know it won't ever be inherited or referenced *dynamically* again. So if `B` refers to itself, since this reference lives on inside `C`, `B` can't ever be replaced with its remote version.

2. If you have
    ```python
    @foo
    @bar
    class C(B):
        pass
    ```
    and you want to `remote`ify the version after `bar` and before `foo`, I think you can do:
    ```python
    @bar
    class C(B):
        pass
    
    C_actor = ray.remote(C)
    C = foo(C)
    ```
    It makes sense to keep two identifiers because you have two versions of `C`.
    Again, any dynamic reference to `C` should be bound statically in `C_actor` to the value it had after `bar`.

    data = mx.symbol.Variable('data')
    # first conv layer
    conv1 = mx.sym.Convolution(data=data, kernel=(5, 5), num_filter=64)
    tanh1 = mx.sym.Activation(data=conv1, act_type="tanh")
    pool1 = mx.sym.Pooling(data=tanh1, pool_type="max", kernel=(2, 2), stride=(2, 2))
    bn1 = mx.sym.BatchNorm(data=pool1)

    # conv2 = mx.sym.Custom(data=conv2, op_type='debug')

    pool2 = mx.sym.Pooling(data=tanh2, pool_type="max", kernel=(2, 2), stride=(2, 2))

    # first fullc layer

We haven't seen this problem occur in practice, so we haven't prioritized it a huge amount.

I'd eventually like to have ways to give hints to the object store about which objects won't be needed anymore and which ones will. For object's that contain numpy arrays, it's already sort of possible to prevent an object from being evicted by calling `x = ray.get(x_id)` on the object ID. As long as `x` is in scope, the object won't be evicted from the local object store (because the numpy arrays are backed by shared memory in the object store). However, I wouldn't want people to rely on that implementation detail.

Detecting repeated evictions and acting based on that is definitely doable. It'd require some thought about which component would be responsible for doing that.

As for concrete proposals, we'd like to be able to diagnose precisely when this is happening and make that information available to the user (this may be doable with a count of how many times each object has been reconstructed).

Or use redis hack as a workaround:

```python
import ray
from ray.utils import random_string

ray.init()



class reflect_id(object):
    def __init__(self, remote):
        self._remote = remote.remote
        self.tag = random_string()

    def remote(self, *args):

        def getter():
            worker = ray.worker.get_global_worker()
            while not worker.redis_client.exists(self.tag):
                pass
            return ray.ObjectID(worker.redis_client.get(self.tag))

        args = (getter,) + args[1:]
        object_id = self._remote(*args)
        worker = ray.worker.get_global_worker()
        worker.redis_client.set(self.tag, object_id.id())
        return object_id


@reflect_id
@ray.remote
def foobar(get_id, x, y):

    print(get_id())
    return x + y


s = foobar.remote(None, 1, 2)
ray.get(s)
print(s)
```

I recently fall into that error. Looks like the API is not returning the `comments` field anymore so the filter is never working. The solution I found was to override the method, and if necessary make a call to `getMediaComments` and get all comments. 

Still there is another problem, that method just return at most 20 comments, and is not ready to get all (i.e. like `getTotalFollowers` does), so again I overrode the method to get all the comments. Find code attached if you needed. Note that I change a bit the method as I found out that a list was returning duplicate elements, so I use a dictionary to avoid duplicates. I also did that on Followers and Friends and now it's a bit more accurate for bigger lists.

```python
def getTotalMediaComments(self, mediaId, amount=None):
    """
    Important:
    Sometimes the `comment_count` doesn't match with the total comments.
    """
    sleep_track = 0
    comments = {}
    next_max_id = ''
    self.getMediaComments(mediaId)
    if "comment_count" in self.LastJson:
        if amount:
            total_comments = min(amount, self.LastJson["comment_count"])
        else:
            total_comments = self.LastJson["comment_count"]

        if total_comments > 20000:
            self.logger.warning(
                "Consider temporarily saving the result of "
                "this big operation. This will take a while.")
    else:
        return False

    with tqdm(
            total=total_comments,
            desc="Getting comments",
            leave=False) as pbar:

        while True:
            self.getMediaComments(mediaId, next_max_id)
            temp = self.LastJson

            try:
                pbar.update(len(temp["comments"]))

                for item in temp["comments"]:
                    comments.update({item['pk']: item})
                    sleep_track += 1
                    if sleep_track >= 2000:
                        sleep_time = randint(120, 180)
                        self.logger.warning(
                            "Waiting %.2f min. due to too many requests." %
                            float(sleep_time / 60))
                        time.sleep(sleep_time)
                        sleep_track = 0

                more_comments = temp["has_more_comments"]
                limit_comments = len(comments.values()) >= total_comments
                if not more_comments or limit_comments:
                    return list(comments.values())[:total_comments]

            except Exception as e:
                self.logger.warning('Exception getting comments: %s' % e)
                return list(comments.values())[:total_comments]

            next_max_id = temp["next_max_id"]
```

There are a few things that aren't clear to me from skimming this, so I'm just going to ask, rather than dig in. Sorry for being lazy! Can you clarify why both `lazy_fail` and `failRetries` is needed? Understanding the meaning of each additional option is extra cognitive load on the users, so we should minimize them if possible. Why not just have one argument, say `retries`, which defaults to zero (and if zero indicates no retries are allowed)?

So instead of:
```Python
            try:
                future.result()
            except Exception as e:
                logger.info("Task[{}]: FAILED with [{}]".format(task_id, e))

                self.tasks[task_id]['fail_count'] += 1

                if not self.lazy_fail:
                    logger.debug("Eager fail, skipping retry logic")
                    raise e

                if self.tasks[task_id]['fail_count'] <= self.fail_retries:
                    logger.debug("Task[{0}]: Retrying".format(task_id))
                    # Set tasks for a retry
                    self.tasks[task_id]['status'] = States.pending

                else:
                    logger.info("Task[{0}]: All retry attempts:{1} have failed".format(task_id,
                                                                                        self.fail_retries))
                    self.tasks[task_id]['status'] = States.failed
```

Consider:
```Python
            try:
                future.result()
            except Exception as e:
                logger.info("Task {} failed with: {}".format(task_id, e))

                self.tasks[task_id]['fail_count'] += 1

                if self.tasks[task_id]['fail_count'] > self.retries:
                    logger.debug("Task {} failed".format(task_id))
                    self.tasks[task_id]['status'] = States.failed
                    raise e
                else:
                    logger.debug("Retrying task {}".format(task_id))
                    self.tasks[task_id]['status'] = States.pending

```
The second version does not differentiate between the case where the retries have run out, and the case where retries were forbidden from the start-- but I'm not sure how useful that information is, since the previous retries will have been logged. In any case, if you want to retain the differentiation, but remove the need for the `lazy_fail` option, you could keep the first version, but change `if not self.lazy_fail:` -> `if self.retries == 0:`.

As to your points:
1. I don't follow, can you clarify?
2. Is `fail` or `max` necessary, would simply `retries` be sufficient?
3. My strong preference would be to allow setting retries per-app rather than only globally.
4. I think it is reasonable not to differentiate between failure types in the first iteration, and open separate issues as needed to describe and discuss more sophisticated features.
/approve

I think some of the difference also may come from use of a historical volcanic forcing in the large ensemble that I set up in the GMD paper. This will dampen the historical temperature response and hence the carbon uptake will be more efficient over the historical period with a volcanic forcing switched on. In my ensemble I am trying to match the historical as best as possible through a variety of metrics (GHG concentrations as well as observed temperature) and then project forward using the RCPs. I found this to be much better when using r0=35 and best estimates of other parameters. We vary this in the large ensemble, so there may be a posterior preference for higher or lower values.

I changed the "except UnicodeDecodeError" to simply "UnicodeError" as I want to catch the encoding errors both in case we get a unicode object as well as a str object.

I also changed it so that the encoded value is what we use for the remainder of the function. This way, the the objects are sure to be strings for the remainder of the method (which makes the pbc driver happy).
